name: Tron
comment:
This is the `Tron a`, which is, similarly to `Html msg` or `Svg msg`, may send your messages into the lifecycle of your application. In this case, it represents your components.

To use Tron in your application, you'll need to specify this function:

    for : Model -> Tron Msg

See `Tron.Builder` for the helpers to define your own interface.

See `WithTron` for the helpers to add `Tron` to your applcation.

# Tron

@docs Tron, Set

# Common helpers

@docs map, mapSet, andThen, with, toUnit

aliases.name: Set
aliases.comment: `Set msg` is just the list of controls' definitions together with their labels.

aliases.name: Tron
aliases.comment:
`Tron a` is the tree of your controls or, recursively, any control in such tree.

To build your interface, use the helpers from the `Tron.Builder` module or any of its variants like
`Tron.Builder.Proxy`, `Tron.Builder.Unit` or `Tron.Builder.String`

values.name: andThen
values.comment: The usual `andThen` function which allows you to change the message type
values.type: (a -> Tron.Tron b) -> Tron.Tron a -> Tron.Tron b

values.name: map
values.comment: The usual `map` function which allows you to substitute the messages sent through the components.

values.name: mapSet
values.comment:
The usual `map` function which allows you to substitute the messages sent through the components in a `Set`. For example, implementation of `Tron.Builder.palette`:

    Builder.choiceBy
        (options
            |> Builder.buttons
            |> List.map (Tron.with (Builder.face << Builder.useColor << Tuple.second))
            |> addLabels Tuple.first
            |> Tron.mapSet Tuple.second
        )
        current
        (\cv1 cv2 ->
            case ( cv1 |> Color.toRgba, cv2 |> Color.toRgba ) of
                ( c1, c2 ) ->
                    (c1.red == c2.red) &&
                    (c1.blue == c2.blue) &&
                    (c1.green == c2.green) &&
                    (c1.alpha == c2.alpha)
        )
        toMsg
    |> cells CS.half

values.name: toUnit
values.comment: Store nothing, but values.

values.name: with
values.comment:
Same as `andThen`, but also gets current component as argument, it gets useful in mapping `Sets` or lists of controls:

    Tron.choiceBy
        (Product.all
            |> List.filter Product.hasIcon
            |> Tron.buttons
            |> List.map (Tron.with (Tron.face << productIcon))
            |> Tron.addLabels Product.getName
        )
        Product.default
        Product.compare
    |> Tron.shape (rows 3)
values.type: (a -> Tron.Tron a -> Tron.Tron a) -> Tron.Tron a -> Tron.Tron a

name: Tron.Builder

values.name: addLabeledPath
values.comment:
Add the path representing the label-based way to reach the
particular control in the GUI tree.
values.type: Tron.Builder.Tron msg -> Tron.Builder.Tron ( List.List String.String, msg )

values.name: addLabels
values.comment:
Convert a list of components to a set by adding labels.

Same as `Builder.toSet`

values.name: addPath
values.comment:
Add the path representing the label-based way to reach the
particular control in the GUI tree.
values.type: Tron.Builder.Tron msg -> Tron.Builder.Tron ( List.List Basics.Int, msg )

values.name: bool
values.comment: `bool` is the alias for `Builder.toggle`

values.name: button
values.comment:
`button` creates a control over a _unit_ `()` value. Type science aside, when you receive the unit value `()` in the handler, it just means that this button was pushed.

    Builder.button <| always DoABang

values.name: buttons
values.comment:
`buttons` is the helper to translate a list of anything to a list of buttons.

It could be useful to pass such list to `choice` or `nest`:

    Builder.choiceBy
        ([ Sine, Square, Triangle, Saw ]
            |> buttons
            |> addLabels waveToString
        )
        model.waveShape
        compareWaves -- sometimes just (==) works, but it's better not to rely on it
        ChangeWaveShape

Or:

    Builder.nest
        ([ Sine, Square, Triangle, Saw ]
            |> buttons
            |> toSet waveToString -- `toSet` is just another name for `addLabels`
            |> handleWith ChangeWaveShape
        )

values.name: cells
values.comment:
Changes cell shape for `nest` and `choice` panels:

    Builder.nest ... |> Buidler.cells single

    Builder.choice ... |> Buidler.shape halfByTwo

    Builder.choice ... |> Buidler.shape halfByHalf

values.name: choice
values.comment:
`choice` defines a list of options for user to choose between. Consider it as `<select>` tag with `<option>`s. When some option is chosen by user, the handler gets the corresponding value. Notice that we ask for `comparable` type here.

    Builder.choice
        ([ 128, 256, 512 ]
            |> buttons
            |> addLabels String.fromInt
        )
        model.bitrate
        ChangeBitrate

*NB*: If you don't want to use `comparable` types, but rather want to specify you own compare function, use `choiceBy`.

*NB*: If you want to add icons to the buttons, use `buttons |> List.map (Tron.map (face << myIcon))`, where `myIcon : a -> Face`, for colors use `[ Color.white, Color.red, Color.yellow, ... ] |> buttons |> List.map (Tron.map (face << useColor))`.

See also: `Builder.choiceBy`, `Builder.strings`, `Builder.palette`, `Style.Shape`, `Style.CellShape`
values.type: Tron.Builder.Set comparable -> comparable -> (comparable -> msg) -> Tron.Builder.Tron msg

values.name: choiceBy
values.comment:
`choiceBy` is identical to `choice`, but asks user for a custom comparison function instead of requiring `comparable` values.

    Builder.choiceBy
        ([ Sine, Square, Triangle, Saw ]
            |> buttons
            |> addLabels waveToString
        )
        model.waveShape
        compareWaves -- sometimes just (==) works, but it's better not to rely on it
        ChangeWaveShape

See also: `Builder.strings`, `Builder.palette`, `Style.Shape`, `Style.CellShape`
values.type: Tron.Builder.Set a -> a -> (a -> a -> Basics.Bool) -> (a -> msg) -> Tron.Builder.Tron msg

values.name: collapse
values.comment:
Forcefully collapse the nesting:

    Builder.nest ... |> Builder.collapse
    Builder.choice ... |> Builder.collapse

values.name: color
values.comment:
`color` creates a control over a color, for the moment it is Hue/Saturation in 2D space, same as `xy`, but with different representation, but we may improve it later. Or you may change it to `choice` with your own palette.

The `Color` type here is from `avh4/elm-color` module.

    Builder.color model.lightColor AdjustColor

values.name: coord
values.comment: `coord` is the alias for `Builder.xy`
values.type: ( Axis.Axis, Axis.Axis ) -> ( Basics.Float, Basics.Float ) -> (( Basics.Float, Basics.Float ) -> msg) -> Tron.Builder.Tron msg

values.name: expand
values.comment:
Forcefully expand the nesting:

    Builder.nest ... |> Builder.expand
    Builder.choice ... |> Builder.expand

values.name: face
values.comment:
Set face for the `button`, `nest`, or `choice`, it can be icon or color:

    Builder.button (always DoABang)
        |> face (iconAt [ "assets", "myIcon.svg" ])

    Builder.nest
        ... |> face (iconAt [ "assets", "myIcon.svg" ])

    Builder.choice
        ... |> face (iconAt [ "assets", "myIcon.svg" ])

    Builder.buttons ...
        |> Tron.map (face << icon)

values.name: float
values.comment:
`float` creates a control over a rational number value, with a minimum, maximum and a step.

    Builder.float { min = 0, max = 44000, step = 1 } myModel.frequency ChangeFrequency

values.name: handleWith
values.comment:
Handle a set of items with a converter of item to a message

    Builder.nest
        ([ Sine, Square, Triangle, Saw ]
            |> buttons
            |> toSet waveToString -- `toSet` is just another name for `addLabels`
            |> handleWith ChangeWaveShape
        )

Alias for `Tron.mapSet`

values.name: icon
values.comment:
Create an `Icon` from its URL or filename.

    import Url.Builder as Url

    Builder.icon
        <| makeUrl <| Url.relative [ "assets", "myicon.svg" ] []

See also: `Builder.iconAt`, `Builder.themedIcon`, `Builder.themedIconAt`

values.name: iconAt
values.comment:
Create an `Icon` using its relative local path.

    Builder.iconAt [ "assets", "myicon.svg" ]

See also: `Builder.themedIconAt`

values.name: input
values.comment:
`input` creates a control over a value which can be translated to `String` and parsed from `String`. It is just a helper over `text` control.

    Builder.input
        (\color ->
            case color of
                Red -> "red"
                Green -> "green"
                Blue -> "blue"
        )
        (\val ->
            case val of
                "red" -> Just Red
                "green" -> Just Green
                "blue" -> Just Blue
                _ -> Nothing
        )
        model.selectedColor
        ChangeColor
values.type: (a -> String.String) -> (String.String -> Maybe.Maybe a) -> a -> (a -> msg) -> Tron.Builder.Tron msg

values.name: int
values.comment:
`int` creates a control over an integer number value, with a minimum, maximum and a step.

    Builder.int { min = 1, max = 8, step = 1 } myModel.octave ChangeOctave
values.type: { min : Basics.Int, max : Basics.Int, step : Basics.Int } -> Basics.Int -> (Basics.Int -> msg) -> Tron.Builder.Tron msg

values.name: labels
values.comment:
`labels` is a helper to create `choice` over the values that could be converted to string/labels and compared using those.

Requires a message that is a fallback for a case when comparison failed.
values.type: (a -> Tron.Property.Label) -> List.List a -> a -> msg -> (a -> msg) -> Tron.Builder.Tron msg

values.name: live
values.comment:
Convert any control to update its value live (i.e. on every change take them from you model)

    Builder.knob ... |> Builder.live

values.name: makeUrl
values.comment: Make URL from String

values.name: nest
values.comment:
`nest` lets you group other controls (including other `nest`ings) under a button which expands a group. Also, this group can be _detached_ if GUI is confugured so.

Handler receives the state of the group, like if it is exapanded or collapsed or detached, but usually it's fine just to make it `always NoOp`.

    Builder.nest
        [
            ( "red"
            , Builder.float { min = 0, max = 255, step = 0.1 } model.red <| AdjustColor Red
            )
        ,
            ( "green"
            , Builder.float { min = 0, max = 255, step = 0.1 } model.blue <| AdjustColor Green
            )
        ,
            ( "blue"
            , Builder.float { min = 0, max = 255, step = 0.1 } model.blue <| AdjustColor Blue
            )
        ]

See also: `Style.Shape`, `Style.CellShape`

values.name: none
values.comment:
Similar to `Cmd.none`, `Sub.none` etc., makes it easier to use expressions in the definition.

For example:

    if user |> User.is Root then
        Builder.button RemoveAllFiles
    else
        Builder.none

values.name: number
values.comment:
`number` is the alias for `Builde.float`.

    Builder.number { min = 0, max = 44000, step = 1 } myModel.frequency ChangeFrequency

values.name: palette
values.comment:
`palette` is a helper to create `choice` over color values.

    Builder.palette
        [ Color.aqua, Color.rouge, Color.vanilla ]
        model.iceCreamColor
        RepaintIceCream
values.type: List.List ( Tron.Property.Label, Color.Color ) -> Color.Color -> (Color.Color -> msg) -> Tron.Builder.Tron msg

values.name: root
values.comment:
Use the root only once, to mark the first visible row of your UI, and put anything else inside.

Actually it is just an alias for the nested row of controls, always expanded.

    for myModel =
        Builder.root
            [
                ( "octave"
                , Builder.int { min = 1, max = 8, step = 1 } myModel.octave ChangeOctave
                )
            ,
                ( "note"
                , Builder.int { min = 1, max = 127, step = 1 } myModel.midiNote ChangeMidiNote
                )
            ,
                ( "shape"
                , Builder.nest

                    [
                        ( "sine"
                        , Builder.button
                            (always <| ChangeShape Sine)
                            |> Builder.face
                                (Builder.icon <| Url.Builder.relative [ "sawwave.svg" ] [])

                        )
                    ,
                        ( "square"
                        , Builder.button
                            (always <| ChangeShape Square)
                            |> Builder.face
                                (Builder.icon <| Url.Builder.relative [ "sawwave.svg" ] [])
                        )
                    ,
                        ( "saw"
                        , Builder.button
                            (always <| ChangeShape Saw)
                            |> Builder.face
                                (Builder.icon <| Url.Builder.relative [ "sawwave.svg" ] [])

                        )
                    ]

                )
            ]

values.name: shape
values.comment:
Changes panel shape for `nest` and `choice` panels:

    Builder.nest ... |> Buidler.shape (cols 2)

    Builder.choice ... |> Buidler.shape (rows 1)

    Builder.choice ... |> Buidler.shape (by 2 3)

values.name: strings
values.comment:
`strings` is a helper to create `choice` over string values.

    Builder.strings
        greekChildrenNames
        model.currentChildName
        ChangeChildName
values.type: List.List String.String -> String.String -> (String.String -> msg) -> Tron.Builder.Tron msg

values.name: text
values.comment:
`text` creates a control over a `String` value.

    Builder.text model.elfName RenameElf

values.name: themedIcon
values.comment:
Create a themed `Icon` from its URL or filename.

    import Url.Builder as Url

    Builder.themedIcon
        <| \theme ->
            makeUrl <| Url.relative [ "assets", "myicon_" ++ Theme.toString theme ++ ".svg" ] []

values.name: themedIconAt
values.comment:
Create a themed `Icon` using its relative local path.

    Builder.themedIconAt
        <| \theme -> [ "assets", "myicon_" ++ Theme.toString theme ++ ".svg" ]

values.name: toChoice
values.comment:
Convert a `nest` control to `choice` control. It can be done
easily by specifying a handler:

    Builder.nest
        ([ Sine, Square, Triangle, Saw ]
            |> buttons
            |> toSet waveToString -- `toSet` is just another name for `addLabels`
            |> handleWith (always NoOp)
        )
    |> toChoice ChangeShapeById

values.name: toKnob
values.comment:
Convert choice control to the knob form:

    Builder.choice ... |> Builder.toKnob

values.name: toSet
values.comment: Convert a list of components to a set by adding labels.

values.name: toSwitch
values.comment:
Convert choice control to a switch by click form:

    Builder.choice ... |> Builder.toSwitch

values.name: toggle
values.comment:
`toggle` creates a control over a boolean value.

    Builder.toggle model.lightOn SwitchLight

values.name: useColor
values.comment:
Create a button face representing a color:

    button (always NoOp) |> face (useColor Color.green)

    [ Color.white, Color.red, Color.yellow ]
        |> buttons
        |> List.map (Tron.with (face << useColor))
        |> addLabels Color.colorToHexWithAlpha

values.name: xy
values.type: ( Axis.Axis, Axis.Axis ) -> ( Basics.Float, Basics.Float ) -> (( Basics.Float, Basics.Float ) -> msg) -> Tron.Builder.Tron msg

name: Tron.Builder.Proxy

values.name: autoHandle
values.comment: The replacement for `handleWith` since we convert everything automatically for Proxy.

values.name: choiceBy
values.type: Tron.OfValue.Set a -> a -> (a -> a -> Basics.Bool) -> Tron.Builder.Proxy.Tron

values.name: int
values.type: { min : Basics.Int, max : Basics.Int, step : Basics.Int } -> Basics.Int -> Tron.Builder.Proxy.Tron

name: Tron.Builder.Unit

values.name: choiceBy
values.type: Tron.Set a -> a -> (a -> a -> Basics.Bool) -> Tron.Builder.Unit.Tron

values.name: dontHandle
values.comment: The replacement for `handleWith` since we don't handle anything for ()

values.name: int
values.type: { min : Basics.Int, max : Basics.Int, step : Basics.Int } -> Basics.Int -> Tron.Builder.Unit.Tron

name: Tron.Control.Value

values.name: encode
values.comment: Encode value to JSON.

values.name: get
values.comment: get proxied value from `Tron`

values.name: getTypeString
values.comment: Get type of the value as string.

values.name: lift
values.comment: convert usual `Tron a` to `Tron.OfValue a`. Please prefer the one from the `Tron.OfValue` module.

values.name: toString
values.comment: Encode value to string (regardless of the type).

name: Tron.Expose.Convert

values.name: evaluate
values.comment: Use current value of the control and apply it to the handler.

values.name: lift
values.comment: Create proxied property. Notice that It will return the `a` disregardless of what `Value` is.

values.name: reflect
values.comment: Extract the value from the control and put it along with a subject of the functor.

values.name: toExposed
toProxied
values.comment:
Store a `Value` together with message, which mirrors a value
as a data type to make it easier to connect with other APIs, where message is not
important, such as `dat.gui`, or send it to ports along with sending it to user.

Use `Builder.map Tuple.first` to get rid of the message if you don't need it.

values.name: toStrExposed
values.comment:
Store a labeled path (such as `honk/color`) to the property and its stringified value,
together with message.

Use `Builder.map Tuple.first` to get rid of the message if you don't need it.
values.type: Tron.Tron msg -> Tron.Tron ( ( Tron.Property.LabelPath, String.String ), msg )

values.name: toUnit
values.comment: Replace the `()` value everywhere within Tron GUI tree, it is useful for truly a lot of cases when you don't care about what are the associated values.

name: Tron.Expose.Data

aliases.name: Ack
aliases.comment: Acknowledge package, which lets the server know the current Client ID.

aliases.name: RawClientId
aliases.comment: Client ID as a JSON value

aliases.name: RawInUpdate
aliases.comment:
The JSON-friendly structure of updates which is received
from the incoming ports by Tron.
aliases.type: { path : Tron.Expose.Data.RawPath, value : Json.Encode.Value, type_ : String.String }

aliases.name: RawOutUpdate
aliases.comment:
The JSON-friendly structure of updates which is sent
to the outgoing ports from Tron.

aliases.name: RawProperty
aliases.comment: The GUI Tree converted to a JSON value.

aliases.name: RawValue
aliases.comment: The JSON-friendly representation of any value.
aliases.type: { path : Tron.Expose.Data.RawPath, value : Json.Encode.Value, stringValue : String.String, labelPath : List.List String.String, type_ : String.String }

name: Tron.OfValue
comment:
This is the `Tron a`, which is, similarly to `Html msg` or `Svg msg`, may send your messages into the lifecycle of your application. In this case, it represents your components.

To use Tron in your application, you'll need to specify this function:

    for : Model -> Tron Msg

See `Tron.Builder` for the helpers to define your own interface.

See `WithTron` for the helpers to add `Tron` to your applcation.

# Tron

@docs Tron, Set

# Common helpers

@docs map, mapSet, andThen, with, toUnit, lift

aliases.name: Set
aliases.comment: `Set a` is just the list of controls' definitions together with their labels.

aliases.name: Tron
aliases.comment:
`Tron a` is the tree of your controls or, recursively, any control in such tree.

To build your interface, use the helpers from the `Tron.Builder` module or any of its variants like
`Tron.Builder.Proxy`, `Tron.Builder.Unit` or `Tron.Builder.String`

values.name: andThen
values.comment: The usual `andThen` function which allows you to update the control with new inner value.
values.type: (a -> Tron.OfValue.Tron b) -> Tron.OfValue.Tron a -> Tron.OfValue.Tron b

values.name: map
values.comment: The usual `map` function which allows you to change the contained value to a new one.

values.name: mapSet
values.comment:
The usual `map` function which allows you to substitute the messages sent through the components in a `Set`. For example, implementation of `Tron.Builder.palette`:

    Builder.choiceBy
        (options
            |> Builder.buttons
            |> List.map (Tron.with (Builder.face << Builder.useColor << Tuple.second))
            |> addLabels Tuple.first
            |> Tron.mapSet Tuple.second
        )
        current
        (\cv1 cv2 ->
            case ( cv1 |> Color.toRgba, cv2 |> Color.toRgba ) of
                ( c1, c2 ) ->
                    (c1.red == c2.red) &&
                    (c1.blue == c2.blue) &&
                    (c1.green == c2.green) &&
                    (c1.alpha == c2.alpha)
        )
        toMsg
    |> cells CS.half

values.name: toUnit
values.comment: Store nothing, but values.

values.name: with
values.comment:
Same as `andThen`, but also gets current component as argument, it gets useful in mapping `Sets` or lists of controls:

    Tron.choiceBy
        (Product.all
            |> List.filter Product.hasIcon
            |> Tron.buttons
            |> List.map (Tron.with (Tron.face << productIcon))
            |> Tron.addLabels Product.getName
        )
        Product.default
        Product.compare
    |> Tron.shape (rows 3)
values.type: (a -> Tron.OfValue.Tron a -> Tron.OfValue.Tron b) -> Tron.OfValue.Tron a -> Tron.OfValue.Tron b

name: Tron.Option

unions.name: PortCommunication
cases: { ack : Tron.Expose.Data.RawProperty -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, transmit : Tron.Expose.Data.RawOutUpdate -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg }
{ transmit : ( List.List String.String, String.String ) -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg }
{ ack : Tron.Expose.Data.Ack -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, transmit : Tron.Expose.Data.RawOutUpdate -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, receive : Platform.Sub.Sub Tron.Expose.Data.RawInUpdate }
{ ack : Tron.Expose.Data.RawProperty -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, receive : Platform.Sub.Sub Tron.Expose.Data.RawInUpdate }

unions.name: RenderTarget

values.name: detachable
values.type: { ack : Tron.Expose.Data.Ack -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, transmit : Tron.Expose.Data.RawOutUpdate -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, receive : Platform.Sub.Sub Tron.Expose.Data.RawInUpdate } -> Tron.Option.PortCommunication msg

values.name: hidden
values.comment:
GUI is hidden. For example, for the case of `dat.gui`, where your interface is on the JS side, but uses Tron definition in Elm.

See `example/DatGui` for details.

values.name: noCommunication
values.comment: No communication with JS

values.name: sendJson
values.type: { ack : Tron.Expose.Data.RawProperty -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, transmit : Tron.Expose.Data.RawOutUpdate -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg } -> Tron.Option.PortCommunication msg

values.name: sendStrings
values.comment:
Send values as strings using given ports:

- `transmit` sends the value and path to it, when value was changed;
values.type: { transmit : ( List.List String.String, String.String ) -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg } -> Tron.Option.PortCommunication msg

values.name: toDebug
values.comment: Render to Debug mode where all the controls are represented as text boxes with information.

values.name: toHtml
values.comment: Render to HTML using given theme (dark/light) and docked at the requested side (see `Tron.Style.Dock`). Most used option!

values.name: toVr
values.comment:
Render to Virtual Reality using given theme (dark/light); Experimental. Uses `a-frame` library for render, so it should be included in your HTML;

See `example/AFrame` for details.

values.name: withDatGui
values.type: { ack : Tron.Expose.Data.RawProperty -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, receive : Platform.Sub.Sub Tron.Expose.Data.RawInUpdate } -> Tron.Option.PortCommunication msg

name: Tron.Random
comment:
Using this module, you may generate the random UI structure if you would ever want to, for testing purposes, for example.

# Generate random UI

@docs generator

See also: `Tron.OfValue`, `WithTron.for`, `Tron.OfValue.lift`

name: Tron.Style.CellShape
comment:
# Cell Shape

Cell Shape is the place it takes in nested panels. Considering the default shape as 1x1 (`Full`), the meaning of each value is:

* `single` — 1x1
* `half` — 0.5x0.5
* `halfByOne` — 0.5x1
* `oneByHalf` — 1x0.5
* `twiceByHalf` — 2x0.5
* `halfByTwice` — 0.5x2
* `twiceByTwice` - 2x2

@docs CellShape, Unit

# Values
@docs default, single, half, halfByOne, oneByHalf, twiceByHalf, halfByTwice, twiceByTwice

# Determine shape
@docs isHorizontal, isVertical, isSquare, isSmallSquare, isLargeSquare

# Other Helpers
@docs numify, units, toString

values.name: default
isHorizontal
isLargeSquare
isSmallSquare
isSquare
isVertical
numify
toString
units
values.comment: Get shape units as ( <horizontal-unit>, <vertical-unit> )

name: Tron.Style.Dock

unions.name: Dock
unions.comment:
`Dock` describes the direction in which GUI is oriented and to which corner or side of the page it is "docked".

If you are familiar with macOS Dock — here we have the similar concept.

values.name: bottomCenter
bottomLeft
bottomRight
center
middleLeft
middleRight
topCenter
topLeft
topRight

name: Tron.Style.PanelShape

values.name: auto
values.comment: Calculate both rows and column numbers automatically, based on the number of cells inside.

values.name: by
values.comment: Specify panel size manually, i.e. how many cells horizontally and how many vertically.

values.name: cols
values.comment: Specify how many cell columns there should be in the panel, and calculate rows number automatically.

values.name: distribute
values.comment: Get numeric size of a panel in cells, and a set of pages required, if there are overflows. Floats, since there could be half-cells.
values.type: Tron.Style.PanelShape.PanelShape -> Tron.Style.CellShape.CellShape -> List.List a -> ( Tron.Pages.Pages (List.List a), Size.SizeF Size.Cells )

values.name: rows
values.comment: Specify how many cell rows there should be in the panel, and calculate columns number automatically.

name: Tron.Style.Theme

unions.name: Theme
unions.comment: Dark or Light theme, each of those could be useful for different situation.

values.name: dark
light
toggle
values.comment: Toggle theme from Light to Dark and vice-versa

name: WithTron
comment:
`WithTron` is the set of functions which wrap Elm `Browser.*` helpers and so
let you easily add your GUI to the usual Elm-driven flow.

Here is the `OneKnob` example:

The only things that is different from usual `Elm` application is `for` function which allows you to build the Tron GUI using current state of the model:

    import WithTron exposing (ProgramWithTron)

    type alias Amount = Float

    type Msg
        = AmountChanged Amount

    type alias Model = Amount

    for : Model -> Tron Msg
    for amount =
        Builder.root
            [
                ( "amount"
                , Builder.float
                    { min = 0, max = 1, step = 0.01 }
                    amount
                    AmountChanged
                )
            ]

    init _ =
        ( 0, Cmd.none )

    view amount =
        Html.text
            <| String.fromFloat amount

    update msg curAmount =
        case msg of

            AmountChanged newAmount ->
                ( newAmount
                , Cmd.none
                )

    subscriptions _ = Sub.none

    main : ProgramWithTron () Model Msg
    main =
        WithTron.element
            (Option.toHtml Dock.center Theme.dark)
            Option.noCommunication
            { for = for
            , init = init
            , view = view
            , update = update
            , subscriptions = subscriptions
            }

*NB*: Please don't forget to add a copy of `src/Tron.css` to your application, or refer to one hosted at GitHub.

For help on how to define your interface with `for` function, see the detailed `Tron.Builder` documentation.

Sometimes  you don't even need the `init`/`view`/`update`, for example to connect GUI to the JavaScript or something remote. In this case the `backed` function lets you define the application which just stores the path-to-value map and nothing else.

More examples are in the `README`, and in the `example/*/Main.elm` modules.

See also: `Tron.Option`, `Tron.Builder`, `Tron.Builder.*`.

There are some special types of GUI Builders that can come in handy if you don't want to use messages, but get them as the type-value pairs or add paths to them or just skip them. All of them doesn't require you to specify handling message so, every function from such `Builder` has one argument less:

- `Tron ()` (`Tron.Builder.Unit`) — do not expose values (keeps them inside);
- `Tron Value` (`Tron.Builder.Proxy`) — store values as a type-value data, see `Tron.Control.Value` for more info;
- `Tron String` (`Tron.Builder.String`) — store value stringified;

Any `Tron a` can be converted to `Tron ( Path, Value )` using `Builder.addPath`, `Builder.addLabeledPath` and/or `Tron.Expose.Convert` helpers.

There is a special `Backed` program, that just stores the stringified values in the `Dict` and is able to send them to the JS, see [Backed](#Backed) section below.

# Program

@docs ProgramWithTron

# Program Wrappers
@docs sandbox, element, document, application

See also: `WithTron.Backed`.

aliases.name: ProgramWithTron
aliases.comment: Adds `Model msg` to the Elm `Program` and so controls all the required communication between usual App and GUI.
aliases.type: Platform.Program flags ( model, Tron.Core.State, Tron.Tron () ) (WithTron.WithTronMsg msg)

values.name: application
values.comment:
Wrapper for `Program.application` with `for` function and `Tron` options.

Example from `Detachable/Main.elm`:

    import WithTron exposing (ProgramWithTron)
    import Tron.Style.Theme as Theme exposing (Theme(..))
    import Tron.Style.Dock as Dock
    import Tron.Expose.Data as Exp
    import Tron.Option as Option

    import Example.Goose.Main as Example
    import Example.Goose.Model as Example
    import Example.Goose.Msg as Example
    import Example.Goose.Gui as ExampleGui

    main : ProgramWithTron () Example.Model Example.Msg
    main =
        WithTron.application
            (Option.toHtml Dock.center Theme.light)
            (Option.detachable
                { ack = ackToWs
                , transmit = sendUpdateToWs
                , receive = receieveUpdateFromWs identity
                }
            )
            { for = ExampleGui.for
            , init = always Example.init
            , view =
                \model ->
                    { title = "Detachable Tron"
                    , body = [ Example.view model ]
                    }
            , update = Example.update
            , subscriptions = always Sub.none
            , onUrlChange = always Example.NoOp
            , onUrlRequest = always Example.NoOp
            }

    port receieveUpdateFromWs : (Exp.RawInUpdate -> msg) -> Sub msg

    port sendUpdateToWs : Exp.RawOutUpdate -> Cmd msg

    port ackToWs : Exp.Ack -> Cmd msg
values.type: Tron.Option.RenderTarget -> Tron.Option.PortCommunication msg -> { for : model -> Tron.OfValue.Tron msg, init : flags -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ), subscriptions : model -> Platform.Sub.Sub msg, view : model -> Browser.Document msg, update : msg -> model -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ), onUrlChange : Url.Url -> msg, onUrlRequest : Browser.UrlRequest -> msg } -> WithTron.ProgramWithTron flags model msg

values.name: document
values.comment:
Wrapper for `Program.document` with `for` function and `Tron` options.

For example:

    import WithTron exposing (ProgramWithTron)
    import Tron.Style.Theme as Theme exposing (Theme(..))
    import Tron.Style.Dock as Dock
    import Tron.Expose.Data as Exp
    import Tron.Option as Option

    main : ProgramWithTron () Example.Model Example.Msg
    main =
        WithTron.document
            (Option.toHtml Dock.center Theme.light)
            (Option.detachable
                { ack = ackToWs
                , transmit = sendUpdateToWs
                , receive = receieveUpdateFromWs identity
                }
            )
            { for = ExampleGui.for
            , init = always Example.init
            , view =
                \model ->
                    { title = "Detachable Tron"
                    , body = [ Example.view model ]
                    }
            , update = Example.update
            , subscriptions = always Sub.none
            }
values.type: Tron.Option.RenderTarget -> Tron.Option.PortCommunication msg -> { for : model -> Tron.OfValue.Tron msg, init : flags -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ), subscriptions : model -> Platform.Sub.Sub msg, view : model -> Browser.Document msg, update : msg -> model -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ) } -> WithTron.ProgramWithTron flags model msg

values.name: element
values.comment:
Wrapper for `Program.element` with `for` function and `Tron` options.

Example from `Basic/Main.elm`

    import Tron.Style.Theme as Theme exposing (Theme(..))
    import Tron.Style.Dock as Dock
    import Tron.Option as Option
    import WithTron exposing (ProgramWithTron)

    import Example.Goose.Main as Example
    import Example.Goose.Model as Example
    import Example.Goose.Msg as Example
    import Example.Goose.Gui as ExampleGui

    main : ProgramWithTron () Example.Model Example.Msg
    main =
        WithTron.element
            (Option.toHtml Dock.center Theme.dark)
            Option.noCommunication
            { for = ExampleGui.for
            , init = always Example.init
            , view = Example.view
            , update = Example.update
            , subscriptions = always Sub.none
            }
values.type: Tron.Option.RenderTarget -> Tron.Option.PortCommunication msg -> { for : model -> Tron.OfValue.Tron msg, init : flags -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ), subscriptions : model -> Platform.Sub.Sub msg, view : model -> Html.Html msg, update : msg -> model -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ) } -> WithTron.ProgramWithTron flags model msg

values.name: sandbox
values.comment:
Wrapper for `Program.sandbox` with `for` function and `Tron` options.

For example:

    import Tron.Style.Theme as Theme exposing (Theme(..))
    import Tron.Style.Dock as Dock
    import Tron.Option as Option
    import WithTron exposing (ProgramWithTron)

    main : ProgramWithTron () Example.Model Example.Msg
    main =
        WithTron.sandbox
            (Option.toHtml Dock.center Theme.dark)
            Option.noCommunication
            { for = ExampleGui.for
            , init = Example.init
            , view = Example.view
            , update = Example.update
            }
values.type: Tron.Option.RenderTarget -> Tron.Option.PortCommunication msg -> { for : model -> Tron.OfValue.Tron msg, init : model, view : model -> Html.Html msg, update : msg -> model -> model } -> WithTron.ProgramWithTron flags model msg

name: WithTron.Backed
comment:
`Backed` programs are the ones that have a permanent storage for the interface values behind the API.

This is mostly useful for the programs that heavily interact with JS side, or even only the Tron GUI is there on Elm side and the main code is in JS. So you do not want to specify your own model or messages, but only the GUI and nothing else.

That way, we only ask for `Tron ()` interface definition, with no specific messages required as handler, and `Tron.Builder.Unit` module has all the useful helpers for this, whose functions require no handlers, since no messages are sent.

See different `Backed...` examples in the `example` folder, and also `ForTiler`.

Some helpers below have the access to such storage using `ValueAt` function being passed to the
lifecycle of the app. This is for the cases when you need, for example, to show/hide some parts of your interface still, w/o specifying any model.

# JSON

@docs byJson, BackedByJson

# Strings

@docs byStrings, BackedByStrings

# Proxy

@docs byProxy, BackedByProxy

# as the Application

@docs byProxyApp, AppBackedByProxy

See also `WithTron.ValueAt`.

aliases.name: AppBackedByProxy
aliases.comment: Program, backed with the proxy value storage.
aliases.type: WithTron.ProgramWithTron flags ( WithTron.Backed.ProxyBackedStorage, model ) ( WithTron.Backed.ProxyBackedMsg, msg )

aliases.name: BackedByJson
aliases.comment: Program, backed with the path-to-value storage.

aliases.name: BackedByStrings
aliases.comment: Program, backed with the path-to-value storage.

values.name: byJson
values.comment:
Special helper, that just asks you for the `Unit`-based interface and some port where to send value updates as JSON packages.

See `Tron.Builder.Unit` for the functions that will help you define the interface that only operates `()` values and so doesn't need any messages.

    import WithTron.Backed exposing (BackedByJson)
    import Tron exposing (Tron)
    import Tron.Builder.Unit exposing (..)
    import Tron.Expose.Data as Exp

    gui : Tron ()
    gui =
        Builder.root
            [
                ( "amount"
                , Builder.float
                    { min = 0, max = 1, step = 0.01 }
                    0
                )
            ]

    port transmit : Exp.RawOutUpdate -> Cmd msg
    port ack : Exp.RawProperty -> Cmd msg

    main : BackedByJson
    main =
        WithTron.Backed.byJson
            (Option.toHtml Dock.middleRight Theme.dark)
            ( ack, transmit )
            gui


See `example/ReportToJsBacked` for more details.

*NB*: Notice that using the GUI that changes its tree structure while running may lead to problems since the ID-based paths then correspond to other controls. In this case, please either prefer  `stringBacked` over `backed` (it uses label-path as a key) or always define currently-invisible controls/nestings with `Builder.none` — it creates the so-called ghost control which has it's own ID but completely hidden from the GUI;
values.type: Tron.Option.RenderTarget -> ( Tron.Expose.Data.RawProperty -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, Tron.Expose.Data.RawOutUpdate -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ) -> Tron.Tron () -> WithTron.Backed.BackedByJson

values.name: byProxy
values.comment:
Currently, the only way (okay, including `byProxyApp`, which is actually the same) to get access to the values in the UI, while using `Backed` way of communication.

See `WithTron.ValueAt` for more information and `example/ForTiler` as the example.
values.type: Tron.Option.RenderTarget -> ( Tron.Expose.Data.RawProperty -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, Tron.Expose.Data.RawOutUpdate -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ) -> (WithTron.ValueAt.ValueAt -> Tron.Tron ()) -> WithTron.Backed.BackedByProxy

values.name: byProxyApp
values.comment:
Get access to the current values in UI, using `Backed` to send values to JS and still having
your own model and application flow, separated from those values.

This is for the case when, indeed, you have some simple `Model`, don't want to bother with storing UI values and/or accessing them using `LabelPath` is enough for you.

See `WithTron.ValueAt` for more information and `example/ForTiler` as the example.
values.type: Tron.Option.RenderTarget -> ( Tron.Expose.Data.RawProperty -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, Tron.Expose.Data.RawOutUpdate -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ) -> { for : WithTron.ValueAt.ValueAt -> model -> Tron.Tron msg, init : flags -> WithTron.ValueAt.ValueAt -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ), subscriptions : WithTron.ValueAt.ValueAt -> model -> Platform.Sub.Sub msg, view : WithTron.ValueAt.ValueAt -> model -> Html.Html msg, update : msg -> WithTron.ValueAt.ValueAt -> model -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ) } -> WithTron.Backed.AppBackedByProxy flags model msg

values.name: byStrings
values.comment:
Special helper, that just asks you for the `Unit`-based interface and some port where to send value updates as `(String, String) pairs, which are labeled path and the stringified value.

See `Tron.Builder.Unit` for the functions that will help you define the interface that only operates `()` values and so doesn't need any messages.

    import WithTron.Backed exposing (BackedByStrings)
    import Tron exposing (Tron)
    import Tron.Builder.Unit exposing (..)

    gui : Tron ()
    gui =
        Builder.root
            [
                ( "amount"
                , Builder.float
                    { min = 0, max = 1, step = 0.01 }
                    0
                )
            ]

    port sendUpdate : ( String, String ) -> Cmd msg

    main : BackedByStringsWithTron
    main =
        WtihTron.Backed.byStrings
            (Option.toHtml Dock.middleRight Theme.dark)
            sendUpdate
            gui


See `example/ReportToJsStringBacked` for more details.
values.type: Tron.Option.RenderTarget -> (( List.List String.String, String.String ) -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg) -> Tron.Tron () -> WithTron.Backed.BackedByStrings

name: WithTron.ValueAt

unions.name: Decoder
unions.comment: The decoder which is able to extract the value.

aliases.name: ValueAt
aliases.comment:
`ValueAt` is the function of type `LabelPath -> Maybe Value`.

Using `ask` and any `Decoder` you don't have to worry what `Value` is, just do:

    valueAt |> ask (xy [ "Goose", "Eye" ])

And get `Maybe (Float, Float)` in response. Same works for any of the decoders below:

    valueAt |> ask (toggle [ "Goose", "Punk" ]) -- returns `Maybe Bool`
    valueAt |> ask (choice [ "Color Scheme", "Product" ]) -- returns `Maybe ItemId`
    valueAt |> ask (choiceOf Products.all [ "Color Scheme", "Product" ]) -- returns `Maybe Product`
        -- NB: Just ensure to use the very same list you used for creating the `choice` in this case
    valueAt |> ask (color [ "Feather", "Color" ]) -- returns `Maybe Color`
    -- and so on...

values.name: action
values.comment: Has a little sense, but still there. Could be named `absurd`. Check, if button was pressed at least once.

values.name: ask
values.comment:
Load value from the storage using the decoder, and if it's there, you'll get it:

    valueAt |> ask (xy [ "Goose", "Eye" ]) -- returns `Maybe (Float, Float)`

values.name: choice
values.comment: Load chosen item ID (which is `Int`) by path. Use `choiceOf` to get the actual value.

values.name: choiceOf
values.comment: Load chosen value by path. NB: Ensure to use the very same list you used for creating the `choice` in this case.

values.name: color
values.comment: Load color value by path.

values.name: map
values.comment: Common `map` function for decoder.

values.name: number
values.comment: Load number value by path: works both for `Builder.int` & `Builder.float`

values.name: text
values.comment: Load text value by path

values.name: toggle
values.comment: Load Toggle value by path. Use helpers from `Builder` to extract it.

values.name: xy
values.comment: Load XY value by path
